title: Chocolate drip decoration
subtitle: Ganache drips on a chilled frosted cake
excerpt: Dark chocolate ganache cooled to the right temperature and applied to the top edge of a chilled, frosted cake. The ganache drips down the sides in controlled runs. The temperature of both the ganache and the cake determines how far the drips travel. A turntable makes the application even.
sourceNotes: The drip cake became a widely imitated cake decoration style in the mid-2010s, originating in contemporary patisserie and spreading rapidly through home baking. The technique uses ganache at a specific viscosity to produce controlled drips down the side of a frosted cake. The ganache formulation and application method described here follows the standard home-baker approach that has become established practice.

recipe.yieldDescription: enough for one 20 cm layer cake
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Ganache can be made ahead and rewarmed gently over a pan of hot water until it reaches drip consistency. Do not overheat; above 35 degrees C it will be too thin.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: no oven; a no-bake decorating technique applied to an already-frosted cake

glossaryTerms.definition: For drips, ganache needs to be between 30 and 35 degrees C: warm enough to flow freely but not so warm that it runs all the way to the board. Too hot and the drips pool at the base. Too cool and they stop too short and look lumpy. Test on the chilled cake before committing: apply one test drip at the back. A drip that runs about halfway down the side is the right consistency. Both the ganache temperature and the cake temperature (it must be cold) affect how far the drips travel.

content.text: Drip decoration is controlled by temperature. The
ganache must be at 30 to 35 degrees C
and the cake must be cold. A warm cake on a warm day will cause drips to run straight to the base. Always test one drip at the back of the cake before applying the full decoration.

Put 150 g finely chopped dark chocolate in a heatproof bowl. Heat 150 ml double cream in a small saucepan until it just comes to a boil. Pour the hot cream over the chocolate and leave for 1 minute, then stir from the centre outwards until smooth and glossy. Leave to cool at room temperature for 15 to 20 minutes, stirring occasionally, until it reaches 30 to 35 degrees C and has the consistency of thick double cream.

Place the chilled, frosted cake on a turntable. Test one drip at the back of the cake to check the ganache consistency. Using a teaspoon or a small icing spatula, push small amounts of ganache over the top edge at intervals, allowing gravity to pull each drip down the side. Vary the length of the drips by using more or less ganache at each point. Once the drips are applied, pour the remaining ganache onto the top of the cake and spread level with an icing spatula. Leave to set before slicing.

items.problem: Drips run all the way to the board and pool at the base
items.solution: The ganache is too warm, or the cake is not cold enough. Cool the ganache further before applying, and make sure the cake has been refrigerated for at least 30 minutes before decorating.

items.problem: Drips are short and lumpy and do not flow down the side
items.solution: The ganache is too cool and too thick. Warm it briefly over a pan of hot water, stir, and retest. Small changes in temperature make a significant difference to the flow.
